comment: Both denote a lower-dimensional, non-natural-boundary part of a host that remains genuinely part of that host — DOLCE's own worked example ("a bump or an edge") uses the same vocabulary BFO's fiat-surface/line examples evoke. The closest lexical and structural pairing in this crosswalk.

Both denote a dependent region whose location is fixed relative to a material/physical entity without being a part of it — BFO's "boundaries... have locations determined in relation to some material entity" and DOLCE's "a hole in a piece of cheese... the shadow of a tree" describe the same not-part-of-host dependent-region pattern.

Both denote a boundary dependent on a material entity, but GFO's has no dimensional (point/line/surface) subdivision the way BFO's does, and lacks a prose definition to compare in detail — `overlap`, not `close-match`, reflects this asymmetric level of documentation.

Recorded explicitly as an unresolved comparison, not a correspondence — TUpper's terms have no prose definition anywhere fetched, so whether they denote the same part-of-host boundary concept as BFO's dimensional fiat classes cannot currently be verified beyond their shared structural role (connection to `poly`/`point`/`surface` via `in`/`part`).
No SUMO correspondence identified; zero occurrences of "Boundary"/"Edge"/"Surface" as defined SUMO classes.
No UFO correspondence identified; zero occurrences of "boundary"/"edge"/"surface" in the 2021 paper's full extracted text.
